meta.source: NIST SP 800-171 DoD Assessment Methodology v1.2.1 (2020-06-24), Annex A
meta.note: Verify against the official document before customer use.

controls.requirement: Limit system access to authorized users, processes acting on behalf of authorized users, and devices (including other systems).

guidance.plain: Every account on your systems belongs to a specific, approved person — no shared logins, no ghosts. If you can't say who 'shopfloor2' is, this control isn't met.
guidance.evidence: User list export from Microsoft 365 / Active Directory with a quarterly review sign-off; onboarding/offboarding checklist.
guidance.quick_win: Export your user list today and disable every account you can't match to a current employee.

controls.requirement: Limit system access to the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute.

guidance.plain: Having an account isn't enough — each person can only perform the transactions and functions their job needs. Roles and permissions, not everyone-an-admin. A shop-floor user shouldn't be able to change security settings or financial records.
guidance.evidence: A role-to-function matrix mapping job roles to permitted system actions; screenshots of group/role membership (M365/AD) showing least-function access; a periodic access review with sign-off.
guidance.quick_win: List your system/app roles and confirm no standard user has admin or cross-department functions they don't need — then remove the extras.

controls.requirement: Control the flow of CUI in accordance with approved authorizations.

guidance.plain: You control where CUI is allowed to move — which systems, people, and networks — and block the paths it shouldn't take (no CUI to personal email or a consumer cloud drive).
guidance.evidence: A data-flow diagram showing where CUI enters, is stored, and leaves; mail-flow / DLP rules restricting CUI destinations; a list of authorized flows.
guidance.quick_win: Write down the few places CUI actually lives and moves, then add one mail-flow or DLP rule blocking the most obvious wrong path (e.g., external auto-forwarding).

controls.requirement: Separate the duties of individuals to reduce the risk of malevolent activity without collusion.

guidance.plain: No single person should control a whole sensitive process end to end. Whoever requests a system change isn't the only one who approves it; whoever manages accounts isn't the sole reviewer of the logs — so one person can't act badly without a second noticing.
guidance.evidence: A duty-separation matrix or a documented example (request vs. approve vs. implement handled by different roles); evidence that admin and audit-review duties aren't the same single person.
guidance.quick_win: Pick your highest-risk system task (creating admin accounts, changing security configs) and require a different second person to approve or review it — and write down who does each part.

controls.requirement: Employ the principle of least privilege, including for specific security functions and privileged accounts.

guidance.plain: Everyone gets the minimum access to do their job and nothing extra. Admin rights only for people who truly need them. Fewer admins, tightly scoped — including for security functions.
guidance.evidence: A list of privileged/admin accounts each with a justification; evidence that local-admin rights are removed from standard user machines; a periodic privilege review.
guidance.quick_win: Pull your admin list today and cut it to the few who genuinely need it; remove local-admin from everyday user laptops.

controls.requirement: Use non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing nonsecurity functions.

guidance.plain: Even your admins use a normal (non-admin) account for everyday work — email, browsing, documents — and switch to their admin account only for admin tasks. That way a phished admin session doesn't hand over the keys.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that admins have separate standard and privileged accounts; a policy requiring non-privileged accounts for routine work.
guidance.quick_win: Give each admin a second, standard account for daily email/web, and reserve the admin account for admin work only.

controls.requirement: Prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions and capture the execution of such functions in audit logs.

guidance.plain: Regular users can't run admin-level functions, and whenever anyone does run a privileged function, it's recorded. Two parts: block it for non-admins, and log it when it happens.
guidance.evidence: Configuration showing standard users can't execute privileged functions; audit-log samples that capture privileged actions.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm standard users can't install software or change system settings, and that your audit logging captures admin actions (turn on unified/admin audit logging).

controls.requirement: Limit unsuccessful logon attempts.

guidance.plain: After a set number of wrong password tries, the account locks or slows down, so someone can't just keep guessing. A simple, standard setting.
guidance.evidence: An account-lockout policy screenshot (threshold and duration) from AD / Entra ID / Intune.
guidance.quick_win: Set an account-lockout threshold (commonly 5-10 attempts) in Entra ID/AD if it isn't already — it's a one-setting change.

controls.requirement: Provide privacy and security notices consistent with applicable CUI rules.

guidance.plain: Systems that handle CUI show a use/consent banner at login stating acceptable use, that activity may be monitored, and the applicable privacy/CUI-handling notice, consistent with applicable CUI rules.
guidance.evidence: A screenshot of the logon banner and the acceptable-use / privacy text it references.
guidance.quick_win: Add a login/consent banner (a Group Policy or Intune setting) with standard acceptable-use, monitoring, and privacy language.

controls.requirement: Use session lock with pattern-hiding displays to prevent access and viewing of data after a period of inactivity.

guidance.plain: Screens lock automatically after a few idle minutes and show a lock screen — not the work — so a walk-by can't read CUI on an unattended machine.
guidance.evidence: A policy/configuration setting the inactivity-lock timeout (e.g., 15 minutes) with a pattern-hiding lock screen, applied via GPO/Intune.
guidance.quick_win: Enforce an automatic screen lock (10-15 minutes) by policy across all machines.

controls.requirement: Terminate (automatically) a user session after a defined condition.

guidance.plain: Sessions end automatically under defined conditions — for example, sign-out after long inactivity or a maximum session length, especially for remote and web sessions. This is more than just locking the screen.
guidance.evidence: Configuration for session timeout/termination (VPN idle disconnect, web-app session timeout, Conditional Access sign-in frequency).
guidance.quick_win: Set an idle-disconnect on your VPN and a session timeout / sign-in frequency on your cloud apps.

controls.requirement: Monitor and control remote access sessions.

guidance.plain: All remote access into your environment goes through approved, monitored channels — you know who is connecting remotely and can see and manage those sessions. If you genuinely permit no remote access of any kind — no VPN, no remote desktop, and no reaching company systems/cloud from outside your offices — it may be marked N/A; but this is uncommon for shops using cloud email/apps, since reaching those from off-site is itself remote access. Document the basis in your SSP and the assessor must agree.
guidance.evidence: A list of approved remote-access methods (VPN, RMM); logs/monitoring of remote sessions; Conditional Access policies governing remote sign-in.
guidance.quick_win: Make sure remote access happens only through one approved, logged method (e.g., VPN with MFA) and turn on its connection logging.

controls.requirement: Employ cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions.

guidance.plain: Remote connections are encrypted in transit (e.g., VPN or TLS) so the session can't be read; for CUI the encryption should use FIPS-validated cryptography. Mark N/A only if you truly permit no remote access (see 3.1.12), you document it, and the assessor agrees.
guidance.evidence: VPN/TLS configuration showing encryption in use for remote paths, the protocol/cipher settings, and evidence the mechanism uses FIPS-validated cryptography (CMVP certificate / FIPS mode enabled).
guidance.quick_win: Confirm every remote-access path rides an encrypted tunnel (a modern VPN or TLS) and retire any plaintext remote protocol.

controls.short_title: Route remote access via control points
controls.requirement: Route remote access via managed access control points.

guidance.plain: Remote users come in through a small number of controlled gateways (a VPN concentrator or managed access point) — not scattered direct connections to individual machines.
guidance.evidence: A network diagram showing remote access funneled through defined gateways; firewall rules that enforce it.
guidance.quick_win: Ensure remote access terminates at one managed gateway, and block direct inbound remote connections to workstations.

controls.requirement: Authorize remote execution of privileged commands and remote access to security-relevant information.

guidance.plain: Doing admin-level work or reaching security-relevant information remotely requires explicit authorization — it isn't something any remote user can do by default.
guidance.evidence: A policy authorizing remote privileged actions; Conditional Access or privileged-access controls restricting remote admin; approval records.
guidance.quick_win: Restrict remote admin to specific authorized accounts and devices (Conditional Access), and document who is approved.

controls.requirement: Authorize wireless access prior to allowing such connections.

guidance.plain: Wireless connections are approved before they're allowed — you decide which devices and users can use Wi-Fi; it isn't open. If you permit no wireless at all, mark N/A and document it.
guidance.evidence: A wireless authorization policy; a controlled SSID with access control (802.1X or a pre-authorized device list); a separated guest network.
guidance.quick_win: Lock corporate Wi-Fi to authorized devices/users (WPA2/3-Enterprise or a device allowlist) and split off any guest network.

controls.requirement: Protect wireless access using authentication and encryption.

guidance.plain: Wi-Fi that touches your environment uses strong authentication and encryption (WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise) — not an open network or a weak shared password. Mark N/A only if no wireless is permitted.
guidance.evidence: Wireless configuration showing WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise (or equivalent) with authentication and encryption enabled, using FIPS-validated cryptographic modules where the wireless carries CUI.
guidance.quick_win: Move corporate Wi-Fi to WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise with per-user/device authentication; retire WEP/WPA-Personal for anything touching CUI.

controls.requirement: Control connection of mobile devices.

guidance.plain: Phones and tablets that connect to your systems are managed and controlled — you decide which devices connect and can enforce security on them. Mark N/A if no mobile devices are permitted to connect.
guidance.evidence: MDM/Intune enrollment showing managed mobile devices; Conditional Access requiring a compliant device.
guidance.quick_win: Require mobile devices to be enrolled and compliant (Intune) before they can reach email or company data.

controls.requirement: Encrypt CUI on mobile devices and mobile computing platforms.

guidance.plain: If CUI can land on a phone, tablet, or laptop, that device's storage is encrypted, so a lost or stolen device doesn't leak data.
guidance.evidence: Encryption enforcement using FIPS-validated encryption (e.g., BitLocker XTS-AES in FIPS mode, or MDM-enforced device encryption) with a compliance report.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on and enforce device encryption (BitLocker / mobile device encryption) via Intune and pull a compliance report.

controls.requirement: Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external systems.

guidance.plain: You verify and control how your systems connect to outside systems — partner networks, personal devices, third-party cloud — deciding which are allowed and what they can do. Note: this control can NOT be placed on a POA&M; it must be met at assessment.
guidance.evidence: A policy defining permitted external connections/services; firewall or Conditional Access limits enforcing it; an approved external-systems list.
guidance.quick_win: Write down which external systems and services are approved to connect, and block or restrict the rest — this one must be met, not deferred.

controls.short_title: Limit portable storage on external systems
controls.requirement: Limit use of portable storage devices on external systems.

guidance.plain: You limit using company USB and portable drives on outside or uncontrolled computers (and vice versa), where they could pick up malware or leak CUI.
guidance.evidence: A removable-media policy that addresses use on external systems; endpoint controls governing USB use.
guidance.quick_win: Add a policy line — and an endpoint control where possible — restricting company portable drives to managed machines only.

controls.requirement: Control CUI posted or processed on publicly accessible systems.

guidance.plain: Nothing containing CUI ends up on publicly accessible systems — your website, social media, or public file shares — and someone reviews and authorizes what goes public. Note: this control can NOT be placed on a POA&M; it must be met at assessment.
guidance.evidence: A policy and review process for public postings; evidence of who authorizes website/social content; confirmation no CUI sits on public shares.
guidance.quick_win: Assign one person to review anything before it goes on the public website or social media, and confirm no CUI is on public shares — must be met, not deferred.

controls.requirement: Ensure that managers, systems administrators, and users of organizational systems are made aware of the security risks associated with their activities and of the applicable policies, standards, and procedures related to the security of those systems.

guidance.plain: Everyone who touches your systems — shop floor, front office, and your admins — gets basic security awareness so they understand the risks (phishing, mishandling CUI) and the policies and procedures they're expected to follow. It has to actually happen and be tracked, not just exist as a document.
guidance.evidence: The security awareness training content; a completion roster or LMS/training-platform report showing who took it and when; the policies, standards, and procedures users are made aware of.
guidance.quick_win: Roll out a short annual security-awareness course (M365, KnowBe4, or similar) to all staff and keep a completion log that names each person and the date.

controls.requirement: Ensure that personnel are trained to carry out their assigned information security-related duties and responsibilities.

guidance.plain: People with specific security-related duties — your IT admin, whoever runs backups, whoever handles incident response — get training tied to those responsibilities, above and beyond the general awareness everyone receives.
guidance.evidence: A list of security-relevant roles and the training each requires; completion records or certificates for those individuals; training content mapped to the assigned duties.
guidance.quick_win: Identify the handful of people with real security duties (admins, backup and incident-response owners) and record the role-specific training each has completed or is scheduled to complete.

controls.requirement: Provide security awareness training on recognizing and reporting potential indicators of insider threat.

guidance.plain: Your awareness training also teaches people to recognize and report insider-threat warning signs — a coworker hoarding or exfiltrating data, unusual access, concerning behavior — and tells them exactly how to report it.
guidance.evidence: Training materials that include an insider-threat module (indicators and reporting); completion records; the reporting procedure or channel employees are told to use.
guidance.quick_win: Add an insider-threat segment (what the indicators are and how to report them) to your existing awareness training and track completion.

controls.requirement: Create and retain system audit logs and records to the extent needed to enable the monitoring, analysis, investigation, and reporting of unlawful or unauthorized system activity.

guidance.plain: Your systems keep records of who did what, when — and you keep those records long enough to investigate an incident months later. Default 30-day retention usually isn't enough.
guidance.evidence: Screenshot of audit log settings and retention period; a sample log pull for one user.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on Microsoft 365 unified audit logging and extend retention as far as your license allows.

controls.requirement: Ensure that the actions of individual system users can be uniquely traced to those users, so they can be held accountable for their actions.

guidance.plain: Your logs tie each action back to a specific individual, not a shared account, so after an event you can say exactly who did what. This depends on everyone having their own unique login — no shared 'admin' or 'shopfloor' accounts.
guidance.evidence: Audit-log samples showing individual usernames on actions; confirmation that shared or generic accounts are eliminated (or individually attributable); the user account list backing it up.
guidance.quick_win: Eliminate shared logins so every logged action maps to one named person, then spot-check a log to confirm actions show individual user IDs.

controls.requirement: Review and update logged events.

guidance.plain: Periodically you review which events you're logging and adjust — confirming you still capture what matters and adding anything you're missing — instead of setting logging once and forgetting it.
guidance.evidence: A documented list of the event types you log; records of a periodic review and update of that list (dates, who reviewed, what changed).
guidance.quick_win: Write down the events you log today, then set a recurring (e.g., annual) review to confirm the list still fits your systems and current threats.

controls.requirement: Alert in the event of an audit logging process failure.

guidance.plain: If your logging stops working — a log fills up, a service dies, a log agent stops reporting — someone gets alerted, so you don't go blind without realizing it.
guidance.evidence: Configuration showing an alert fires on an audit/logging process failure (SIEM or log-agent health alert, log-volume-full alert); a sample alert or the notification setting.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on a logging-health or failure alert in your SIEM or logging tool (or at minimum a disk-full alert on the log volume) so an outage notifies someone.

controls.requirement: Correlate audit record review, analysis, and reporting processes for investigation and response to indications of unlawful, unauthorized, suspicious, or unusual activity.

guidance.plain: You don't review each system's logs in isolation — you bring logs together and correlate across them to spot suspicious activity, then act on what you find. In practice this is what a SIEM or a centralized log review provides.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of centralized/correlated log review (SIEM dashboards, correlation rules or alerts); records of investigation and response to flagged activity; who performs the review and how often.
guidance.quick_win: Aggregate your key logs into one place (a SIEM, or centralized Microsoft 365/Defender review) and set a few correlation alerts so cross-system activity is reviewed together, not source by source.

controls.requirement: Provide audit record reduction and report generation to support on-demand analysis and reporting.

guidance.plain: You can take raw logs and quickly filter, search, and generate readable reports on demand — without altering the original records — so investigating an event doesn't mean digging through raw log files by hand for days.
guidance.evidence: The audit tool's search and report-generation capability (SIEM queries and reports, or the Microsoft 365 audit-log search/export); a sample generated report.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm your logging tool can search and export a report on demand (most SIEMs and the M365 audit search can), then save one sample report as proof of the capability.

controls.requirement: Provide a system capability that compares and synchronizes internal system clocks with an authoritative source to generate time stamps for audit records.

guidance.plain: All your systems sync their clocks to one authoritative time source (such as NTP) so log timestamps line up across machines — which is essential for reconstructing the order of events during an investigation.
guidance.evidence: Time-synchronization configuration pointing systems to an authoritative source (the domain-controller time hierarchy, or a trusted NTP server); evidence that timestamps are consistent across systems.
guidance.quick_win: Point your servers and workstations at a single authoritative time source (your domain controller or a trusted NTP server) so log timestamps stay in sync.

controls.requirement: Protect audit information and audit logging tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion.

guidance.plain: Your logs and the tools that manage them are protected from tampering — ordinary users, and even most admins, can't read, alter, or delete audit records. Getting logs off the source system into a protected central store is the usual way to achieve this.
guidance.evidence: Access-control settings on log storage and logging tools showing restricted permissions; evidence logs are forwarded to a protected/central repository; that modification and deletion are prevented or tightly restricted.
guidance.quick_win: Forward logs to a central store where the people being logged can't edit them, and lock down file and tool permissions so audit records can't be modified or cleared by regular users.

controls.short_title: Limit audit management to few admins
controls.requirement: Limit management of audit logging functionality to a subset of privileged users.

guidance.plain: Only a small, specific set of admins can manage the audit logging itself — turning logging on or off, changing what's captured, or clearing logs. Regular users and even other admins can't touch the audit configuration.
guidance.evidence: The list of accounts or roles authorized to manage audit logging; permission settings showing audit-management rights are restricted to that subset.
guidance.quick_win: Restrict audit and log-management rights to one or two named admins, and confirm no one else can change the logging settings or clear the logs.

controls.requirement: Establish and maintain baseline configurations and inventories of organizational systems (including hardware, software, firmware, and documentation) throughout the respective system development life cycles.

guidance.plain: A written inventory of every computer, server, and piece of software you own, plus a documented 'standard build' for how a new machine gets set up.
guidance.evidence: Asset inventory (export from Intune/RMM counts); a one-page standard configuration document.
guidance.quick_win: Export the device list from whatever management tool you have — that's the skeleton of your inventory.

controls.requirement: Establish and enforce security configuration settings for information technology products employed in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You don't run machines on factory defaults — there's a hardening checklist (disable what you don't use, enforce screen lock, etc.) and it's actually applied.
guidance.evidence: Group Policy / Intune configuration profiles; a hardening checklist referencing a benchmark like CIS.
guidance.quick_win: Adopt a published small-business hardening baseline instead of writing your own from scratch.

controls.requirement: Track, review, approve or disapprove, and log changes to organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You run changes to your systems through a process instead of making them on the fly: proposed changes (patches, new software, config or network changes) get logged, reviewed, and either approved or rejected before they happen. The log shows what changed, who asked, who decided, and when.
guidance.evidence: A change-management policy or procedure; a change log or ticket queue with individual change records showing the request, the reviewer, the approve/disapprove decision, and the date; approval sign-offs (or CAB notes) for significant changes.
guidance.quick_win: Start a simple change log today (a shared ticket queue or spreadsheet) and require that every significant system change is recorded there with who requested it, who approved it, and the date.

controls.requirement: Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation.

guidance.plain: Before a change goes in, someone actually thinks about whether it could weaken security — opening a port, disabling a control, or introducing a new vulnerability. This is a review step inside your change process, not a separate program.
guidance.evidence: Change records that include a security-impact note or field completed before approval; a procedure requiring security-impact analysis prior to implementation; evidence changes are validated in a test/staging environment where practical.
guidance.quick_win: Add a "security impact?" line to your change form or ticket template so every change is checked for its security effect before anyone approves it.

controls.requirement: Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Only specific, authorized people can actually make changes to your systems — both logically (who holds the admin rights to change configurations) and physically (who can get to the servers and network gear). Those restrictions are written down and enforced, not just understood.
guidance.evidence: A documented list of who is authorized to make system changes; privileged/admin group membership showing that list is enforced; physical access controls to server and network equipment (locked room/rack, badge logs); a policy stating the change-related access restrictions.
guidance.quick_win: Cut change/configuration rights down to a named short list of admins, confirm the servers and network gear sit behind locked physical access, and write down exactly who is allowed to make changes.

controls.requirement: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring organizational systems to provide only essential capabilities.

guidance.plain: Your systems provide only the capabilities they actually need to do their job — unnecessary software, features, roles, and services are turned off or removed. A smaller footprint means fewer things an attacker can use.
guidance.evidence: A hardening baseline or checklist that identifies and disables nonessential features; a documented list of the essential functions/software for each system type; configuration profiles (Intune/GPO) or CIS-benchmark settings showing nonessential capabilities are off.
guidance.quick_win: Take your standard workstation build and disable or remove the preinstalled apps, roles, and Windows features you don't use, then bake that into your baseline so new machines start locked down.

controls.requirement: Restrict, disable, or prevent the use of nonessential programs, functions, ports, protocols, and services.

guidance.plain: This is the concrete side of least functionality: the nonessential ports, protocols, and services are actually closed or disabled, and unneeded programs are blocked from running. You know which ports/protocols/services you truly need and have shut off the rest (for example, disabling SMBv1 and Telnet, closing unused inbound ports).
guidance.evidence: A documented list of the required/allowed ports, protocols, and services with a business justification for each; host/firewall configuration showing the others are disabled or blocked; evidence that legacy protocols (SMBv1, Telnet, etc.) are turned off.
guidance.quick_win: Write down the ports, protocols, and services you actually need, then disable a known-bad one right now (e.g., SMBv1) and close unused inbound ports on the host firewall.

controls.requirement: Apply deny-by-exception (blacklisting) policy to prevent the use of unauthorized software or deny-all, permit-by-exception (whitelisting) policy to allow the execution of authorized software.

guidance.plain: You control which software is allowed to run on your machines. There are two approaches: deny-by-exception (a blocklist of known-bad software — the weaker option), or the stronger deny-all / allow-by-exception model (allowlisting), where only approved software can execute and everything else is blocked by default. NIST and assessors treat allowlisting as the stronger, preferred implementation, and this is a high-value 5-point control, so an enforced allowlist is worth the effort.
guidance.evidence: A policy stating which approach you use (and, ideally, that you allowlist); the enforcement configuration — AppLocker, Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC), or a third-party allowlisting product — showing rules set to actively enforce, not audit-only; the approved-software list the rules are built from; and logs showing execution actually being allowed or blocked.
guidance.quick_win: Deploy AppLocker or Windows Defender Application Control, start it in audit mode to learn what legitimately runs, then move it to enforce — audit-only mode doesn't satisfy the control. Either an enforced blocklist or an enforced deny-all / allow-by-exception ruleset meets the requirement, but allowlisting is the stronger implementation and worth the effort on this 5-point control.

controls.requirement: Control and monitor user-installed software.

guidance.plain: Regular users can't freely install whatever software they want. There's a rule for what may be installed and a way to enforce and watch it — in practice, standard users don't have local-admin rights (so they can't install), and you track what actually ends up on machines.
guidance.evidence: A policy governing user-installed software; evidence that standard users lack local-admin/install rights; software inventory or monitoring from Intune/RMM/endpoint tooling that detects and reports installed applications.
guidance.quick_win: Remove local-admin from standard user accounts so they can't install software, and use your management tool's software inventory to monitor what's installed and flag anything unexpected.

controls.requirement: Identify system users, processes acting on behalf of users, and devices.

guidance.plain: Every user, every automated process acting for a user, and every device on your systems has a unique identifier so you can tell exactly who or what is connecting. No shared or anonymous IDs, and service/automation accounts are named and accounted for too.
guidance.evidence: A user/account list from Entra ID or Active Directory showing unique per-person accounts; an inventory of service accounts and their purpose; a device inventory (Intune/RMM) showing devices are identified on the network.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm every login account maps to one named person or one documented service/device, and rename or remove any generic shared IDs like 'shopfloor' or 'shipping'.

controls.requirement: Authenticate (or verify) the identities of users, processes, or devices, as a prerequisite to allowing access to organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Before any user, process, or device gets access, its claimed identity is actually proven — a password, certificate, or other authenticator is verified, not just an identifier typed in. Identification (3.5.1) says who you are; authentication proves it, and it must happen first.
guidance.evidence: Authentication configuration for systems handling CUI (Entra ID/AD password and sign-in policy); evidence that devices/services authenticate (certificates, machine accounts, API keys); no systems allowing anonymous or unauthenticated access to CUI.
guidance.quick_win: Verify no system, share, or app that touches CUI allows blank-password or anonymous access, and that every account requires a verified credential to sign in.

controls.requirement: Use multifactor authentication for local and network access to privileged accounts and for network access to non-privileged accounts.

guidance.plain: Multifactor authentication for everyone — not just email. Partial credit exists: MFA for admins and remote users only loses 3 points instead of 5. Full credit requires the general workforce too.
guidance.evidence: MFA status export from your identity provider showing per-user enforcement.
guidance.quick_win: Enforce MFA for admin and remote accounts this week (that alone moves you from −5 to −3), then roll out to everyone.

controls.requirement: Employ replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.

guidance.plain: Authentication over the network resists replay — a captured login can't simply be recorded and replayed to get in. In practice this comes free from the modern protocols you already use (Kerberos, TLS-protected logins, WS-Fed/SAML/OAuth to Entra ID), which use nonces or challenge-response; the risk is legacy protocols that don't.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that network authentication uses replay-resistant protocols (Kerberos, TLS, modern federation to Entra ID); configuration showing legacy/weak authentication (NTLMv1, unencrypted LDAP, basic auth) is disabled.
guidance.quick_win: Disable legacy authentication in Entra ID and turn off NTLMv1/insecure LDAP on-prem so all network logons ride replay-resistant protocols.

controls.requirement: Prevent reuse of identifiers for a defined period.

guidance.plain: Once an account identifier is used, you don't hand that same identifier to a different person for a defined waiting period, so old activity can't be confused with a new user. Usernames and account IDs aren't recycled right away.
guidance.evidence: A written account-management policy stating identifiers are not reused for a defined period; naming-convention documentation; evidence that departed users' identifiers are retired rather than reassigned.
guidance.quick_win: Add a line to your account policy setting how long an identifier stays retired before reuse (a common choice is not reusing it at all, or 12+ months) and follow a unique naming convention so IDs aren't recycled.

controls.requirement: Disable identifiers after a defined period of inactivity.

guidance.plain: Accounts that sit unused for a defined stretch get disabled — ideally automatically, but a documented recurring review works too — so dormant logins can't be quietly abused. This is separate from offboarding — it catches accounts everyone forgot about.
guidance.evidence: Configuration or a documented procedure that disables accounts after a defined inactivity period; a report of stale/last-sign-in dates from Entra ID/AD showing action taken on inactive accounts.
guidance.quick_win: Pull a last-sign-in report and disable accounts idle past your defined threshold (commonly 35-90 days), then set a recurring review or automation to keep doing it.

controls.requirement: Enforce a minimum password complexity and change of characters when new passwords are created.

guidance.plain: New passwords have to meet a minimum complexity rule and, when changed, differ from the old one by a set number of characters. The point is that passwords aren't trivially weak or barely-changed reuses.
guidance.evidence: A password policy screenshot from Entra ID / Active Directory / Intune showing minimum length and complexity settings; the documented complexity standard.
guidance.quick_win: Set and enforce a password policy (a solid modern baseline is a longer minimum length plus banned common/breached passwords via Entra Password Protection) so weak passwords are rejected at creation.

controls.requirement: Prohibit password reuse for a specified number of generations.

guidance.plain: People can't cycle back to a recent old password — the system remembers a set number of past passwords and blocks their reuse. This stops the 'change it then change it right back' trick.
guidance.evidence: Password-history / enforce-password-history setting in Entra ID / AD Group Policy showing the number of remembered generations.
guidance.quick_win: Set the password-history value in your identity system (commonly 24 remembered passwords) so recent passwords can't be reused.

controls.requirement: Allow temporary password use for system logons with an immediate change to a permanent password.

guidance.plain: When you issue a temporary password — for a new hire or a reset — the user is forced to change it to their own permanent one the first time they log in. The temporary credential is never left in place as a standing password.
guidance.evidence: Configuration or procedure requiring a forced password change at first logon (the 'user must change password at next sign-in' flag); help-desk reset process documentation.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on 'require password change at next sign-in' for every new account and every admin-initiated reset so temporary passwords can't linger.

controls.requirement: Store and transmit only cryptographically-protected passwords.

guidance.plain: Passwords are never kept or sent in readable form — at rest they're stored only as salted one-way hashes, and in transit they only cross channels that are encrypted (for example the login page and directory traffic ride TLS). If a database or a captured packet would reveal a usable password, this fails. Where cryptography is protecting CUI, it must be FIPS-validated (3.13.11); the salted-hash and encrypted-transport mechanisms themselves should likewise use validated cryptography.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that credential stores hash passwords (platform/IdP documentation confirming salted one-way hashing — e.g., Entra ID / Active Directory); configuration showing authentication traffic is encrypted (LDAPS instead of plain LDAP, TLS on login portals and app sign-in); confirmation that no application stores or emails passwords in plaintext, and CMVP certificate references where the cryptography protects CUI (3.13.11).
guidance.quick_win: Hunt down and fix any place a password is stored or sent in the clear — replace plain LDAP with LDAPS, force TLS on every login page and app, and confirm no app database or spreadsheet holds plaintext passwords.

controls.requirement: Obscure feedback of authentication information.

guidance.plain: When someone types a password or other authenticator, the system hides it — masked dots instead of visible characters, and no error message that reveals whether the username or the password was the wrong part. This keeps shoulder-surfers and attackers from learning credentials.
guidance.evidence: Screenshots showing password fields are masked at login; evidence that authentication failure messages are generic (they don't reveal which field was wrong); configuration of any custom login screens.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm every login screen masks the password as it's typed and returns a generic 'sign-in failed' message rather than saying whether the username or password was incorrect.

controls.requirement: Establish an operational incident-handling capability for organizational systems that includes preparation, detection, analysis, containment, recovery, and user response activities.

guidance.plain: When something bad happens, you have a written plan: who's in charge, who you call, how you contain it, how you recover. A plan that exists only in someone's head scores zero.
guidance.evidence: A written incident response plan with named roles; notes from at least one walkthrough or tabletop exercise.
guidance.quick_win: Draft a two-page IR plan from a free template and put real names and phone numbers in it.

controls.requirement: Track, document, and report incidents to designated officials and/or authorities both internal and external to the organization.

guidance.plain: When you have a security incident, you track it through to closure, document what happened, and report it to the right people — internally (management/leadership) and externally. For defense contractors this external piece is specific: DFARS 252.204-7012 requires reporting a cyber incident that affects CUI to DoD through DIBNet (dibnet.dod.mil) within 72 hours of discovery.
guidance.evidence: An incident tracking log/register showing each incident's status, handling, and dates; the documented incident reports themselves; a reporting procedure naming the internal recipients and the external path — including the DoD DIBNet 72-hour reporting requirement for incidents affecting CUI; and evidence you can actually file (a DoD-approved medium-assurance PKI certificate, which DIBNet reporting requires).
guidance.quick_win: Stand up an incident log/register and write down the reporting steps — who is notified internally and the exact external path (report to DoD via dibnet.dod.mil within 72 hours) — then obtain the medium-assurance PKI certificate DIBNet needs now, so you're not scrambling for it during a real incident.

controls.requirement: Test the organizational incident response capability.

guidance.plain: It's not enough to have an incident response plan — you exercise it. A tabletop or walkthrough where the team runs a realistic scenario surfaces the gaps (missing contacts, unclear roles, no backups) before a real incident does, and you keep a record that you tested.
guidance.evidence: Dated records of an incident-response test or tabletop (the scenario used, who participated, the date); after-action notes or lessons learned; and any updates you made to the IR plan as a result of the test.
guidance.quick_win: Run a one-hour tabletop this quarter walking the team through a realistic scenario (e.g., ransomware or a phished-admin account), then write down the date, who took part, and the fixes you identified.

controls.requirement: Perform maintenance on organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You actually perform maintenance on your systems — servers, workstations, network gear — in a controlled, deliberate way rather than ad hoc. That means scheduled upkeep, following vendor and security guidance, and keeping a record of what was done and by whom.
guidance.evidence: A maintenance policy/procedure; maintenance logs or tickets showing scheduled and performed maintenance (dates, systems, who did the work); vendor/service records.
guidance.quick_win: Start a simple maintenance log (even a shared spreadsheet) and record the next few maintenance actions — what, when, and who — so there is a record the activity is controlled.

controls.requirement: Provide controls on the tools, techniques, mechanisms, and personnel used to conduct system maintenance.

guidance.plain: The tools, techniques, and people used to maintain your systems are controlled — you approve and monitor diagnostic/repair tools (they're a common way malware walks in), and you vet and authorize whoever performs maintenance. This applies to both in-house staff and outside technicians touching systems that handle CUI.
guidance.evidence: A list of approved maintenance tools with approval records; a policy governing maintenance tools and personnel; records that maintenance personnel (including third parties) are authorized/vetted; monitoring of maintenance-tool use.
guidance.quick_win: Write down which maintenance tools are approved and who is authorized to perform maintenance (including your outside IT/vendor), and require approval before any new tool or technician touches CUI systems.

controls.requirement: Ensure equipment removed for off-site maintenance is sanitized of any CUI.

guidance.plain: Before any equipment that held CUI leaves your building for off-site repair or warranty service, the CUI is removed — wiped or the storage sanitized — so the vendor never receives your CUI. If a drive can't be sanitized, remove it before the unit ships.
guidance.evidence: A sanitization policy for off-site maintenance; a log of equipment sanitized before leaving (asset, date, method, who verified); certificates or records tied to specific units sent out for service.
guidance.quick_win: Add a rule to your maintenance process: no CUI-bearing device leaves for repair until its storage is wiped or removed, and log each instance.

controls.requirement: Check media containing diagnostic and test programs for malicious code before the media are used in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Any media carrying diagnostic or test programs — a technician's USB stick, a vendor's utility disk, a downloaded firmware/diagnostic tool — is scanned for malicious code before it's connected to your systems. Maintenance media is a classic malware entry point, so it gets checked first, every time.
guidance.evidence: A policy requiring malware scanning of diagnostic/test media before use; scan records or logs; endpoint controls that scan removable media on connection.
guidance.quick_win: Require every diagnostic/test USB or disk (including a vendor's) to be malware-scanned before it touches a system, and configure endpoints to auto-scan removable media on insertion.

controls.requirement: Require multifactor authentication to establish nonlocal maintenance sessions via external network connections and terminate such connections when nonlocal maintenance is complete.

guidance.plain: Any maintenance done remotely over an external network — a vendor or admin dialing in through RMM, VPN, or a remote-desktop tool — requires multifactor authentication to start the session, and the connection is torn down as soon as the maintenance is finished (not left open). When that remote maintenance channel protects CUI, the encryption behind it must be FIPS-validated cryptography (see 3.13.11).
guidance.evidence: MFA enforcement on remote-maintenance/RMM/VPN access (configuration or identity-provider export); evidence sessions terminate when work is complete (session logs, idle/disconnect settings); for the encrypted channel protecting CUI, the FIPS 140 CMVP validation for the module (see 3.13.11).
guidance.quick_win: Require MFA on every remote-maintenance path (RMM, VPN, remote desktop) and confirm sessions close out when the job is done rather than staying connected.

controls.requirement: Supervise the maintenance activities of maintenance personnel without required access authorization.

guidance.plain: When someone does maintenance but hasn't been vetted for access to your CUI systems — an outside repair tech, a contractor without a background check — an authorized person supervises them the whole time they're on site or connected. No unescorted, unvetted hands on the equipment.
guidance.evidence: A policy requiring supervision of maintenance personnel lacking access authorization; visitor/escort logs tied to maintenance visits; records naming the authorized supervisor for each such activity.
guidance.quick_win: Set a standing rule that any un-vetted maintenance worker is escorted and supervised by an authorized employee for the entire visit, and log who supervised.

controls.requirement: Protect (i.e., physically control and securely store) system media containing CUI, both paper and digital.

guidance.plain: Media that holds CUI — USB drives, external disks, backup tapes, and printed documents — is physically controlled and stored securely (a locked cabinet or a secured room), not left sitting on desks or open shelves. This covers both paper and digital media.
guidance.evidence: A media protection policy; a description or photos of the locked storage used for CUI media and printouts; an inventory of media that contains CUI.
guidance.quick_win: Put CUI-bearing drives and printouts in a locked cabinet or secured room today, and write down where CUI media is stored.

controls.short_title: Limit access to CUI on media
controls.requirement: Limit access to CUI on system media to authorized users.

guidance.plain: Only authorized people can reach the CUI on your media — enforced both physically (who can get to the locked storage) and logically (who can open the files). Someone without a need to know doesn't get access.
guidance.evidence: Access permissions on the shares/folders holding CUI; a list of who has keys or badge access to the media storage; authorization records tying access to a need.
guidance.quick_win: Review who can open the folders and drives holding CUI and who has keys to the media cabinet, and remove anyone without a genuine need.

controls.requirement: Sanitize or destroy system media containing CUI before disposal or release for reuse.

guidance.plain: Before a computer, drive, or copier leaves your building — sold, recycled, returned off lease — the data on it is wiped or the drive is destroyed, and you keep a record.
guidance.evidence: Wipe/destruction log; certificates of destruction from your recycler.
guidance.quick_win: Start a simple disposal log today; ask your recycler for certificates going forward.

controls.requirement: Mark media with necessary CUI markings and distribution limitations.

guidance.plain: Media and documents containing CUI carry the required CUI markings and any distribution or handling limitations, so anyone who handles them knows what they are and how they must be protected. Follow the CUI marking rules — the CUI banner marking, category, and any dissemination controls.
guidance.evidence: Examples of marked media and documents (CUI banner markings, labels on drives); a marking procedure that references the CUI marking standard.
guidance.quick_win: Start labeling CUI files and removable media with the "CUI" banner marking, and write a short procedure pointing to the CUI marking rules.

controls.requirement: Control access to media containing CUI and maintain accountability for media during transport outside of controlled areas.

guidance.plain: When CUI media leaves your controlled areas — hand-carried, shipped, or taken off-site — you control who has custody of it and keep a record so it stays accountable the whole way. You can say who held it and where it went.
guidance.evidence: A transport or chain-of-custody log; shipping/tracking records for CUI media; a policy governing how media is controlled during transport.
guidance.quick_win: Start a simple custody log for any CUI media that leaves the building (who took it, when, and where) and use trackable shipping for anything mailed.

controls.short_title: Encrypt CUI on media in transport
controls.requirement: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of CUI stored on digital media during transport unless otherwise protected by alternative physical safeguards.

guidance.plain: CUI on digital media (USB drives, external disks, backup media) that travels outside your controlled areas is encrypted so it can't be read if the media is lost or stolen — unless it's protected another physical way, such as a locked, guarded courier. When you use encryption to protect CUI, it must be FIPS-validated — that requirement comes from 3.13.11.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that digital media in transport is encrypted (encrypted USB / BitLocker To Go, encrypted backup media), including the module's FIPS 140 CMVP validation certificate number; or documentation of the alternative physical safeguards used instead.
guidance.quick_win: Encrypt any USB or external drive that carries CUI off-site (e.g., BitLocker To Go), and record the module's CMVP certificate number so you can show the encryption is FIPS-validated (3.13.11).

controls.requirement: Control the use of removable media on system components.

guidance.plain: USB sticks and external drives don't just get plugged in freely. You either block removable media by policy and technical control, or you control which specific devices are allowed.
guidance.evidence: Endpoint policy screenshot showing removable-media restrictions; written removable media policy.
guidance.quick_win: If you can't manage USB devices yet, block them outright — blocking fully satisfies this control.

controls.requirement: Prohibit the use of portable storage devices when such devices have no identifiable owner.

guidance.plain: No plugging in USB sticks or portable drives that have no identifiable owner — the "found in the parking lot" drive is exactly what this blocks. Every portable storage device in use is one you can trace to a responsible person.
guidance.evidence: A policy prohibiting the use of ownerless portable storage; endpoint control settings that block unknown/unapproved USB devices; an inventory tying devices to owners.
guidance.quick_win: Add a clear policy line banning use of any USB or portable drive without an identifiable owner, and where possible enforce it with an endpoint control that blocks unknown devices.

controls.requirement: Protect the confidentiality of backup CUI at storage locations.

guidance.plain: Backups that contain CUI are protected wherever they're stored — on-site, off-site, or in the cloud — so anyone who reaches the backup can't read the CUI. Encryption is the usual method; when you use encryption to protect CUI, it must be FIPS-validated (that requirement comes from 3.13.11).
guidance.evidence: Evidence that CUI backups are encrypted or otherwise access-controlled at their storage location (backup or cloud-backup encryption settings), including the FIPS 140 CMVP certificate number for the module used; a description of where backups live and who can access them.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on encryption for your CUI backups (on-site and cloud), confirm access is restricted, and record the module's CMVP certificate number to show the encryption is FIPS-validated (3.13.11).

controls.requirement: Screen individuals prior to authorizing access to organizational systems containing CUI.

guidance.plain: Before you give someone access to systems that hold CUI, you screen them — typically a background check appropriate to the role — so you're not handing sensitive data to an unvetted person. This applies to new hires and to anyone (including contractors) getting CUI access.
guidance.evidence: A personnel screening policy; records that background screening was completed before CUI access was granted (dates tied to access-grant dates); onboarding checklist linking screening to access.
guidance.quick_win: Add a screening step to onboarding so CUI-system access isn't granted until the background check is complete, and keep a record tying the two together.

controls.requirement: Ensure that organizational systems containing CUI are protected during and after personnel actions such as terminations and transfers.

guidance.plain: When someone leaves or changes roles, your CUI systems are protected during and after that change — accounts are disabled promptly, credentials revoked, and company equipment and CUI recovered. The classic gap is a terminated employee whose account still works days later.
guidance.evidence: A termination/transfer (offboarding) checklist covering account disablement, credential revocation, and equipment/CUI return; records of completed offboarding with timestamps; evidence that access was removed at or before departure.
guidance.quick_win: Build an offboarding checklist that disables accounts and revokes access on the person's last day (or role-change date) and recovers their devices — then run it every time.

controls.requirement: Limit physical access to organizational systems, equipment, and the respective operating environments to authorized individuals.

guidance.plain: Only authorized people can physically get to your systems and the areas that house them — the server room, network closet, and workstations handling CUI. Locks, badges, or keys keep everyone else out.
guidance.evidence: A physical access control policy; a list of individuals authorized for sensitive areas; evidence of locks or badge readers on the server room and CUI areas; the badge/key issuance record.
guidance.quick_win: Lock the server/network room and limit keys or badge access to the few people who need it, and write down who is authorized.

controls.requirement: Protect and monitor the physical facility and support infrastructure for organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You physically protect and keep an eye on the facility and its supporting infrastructure (power, network cabling, HVAC where it matters) — for example alarms, cameras, or monitored entry — so intrusion or tampering gets noticed rather than going undetected.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of facility protection and monitoring (alarm system, surveillance cameras, monitored entry logs); a description of how the facility and support infrastructure are protected.
guidance.quick_win: Make sure the facility has working, monitored protection — an alarm and/or cameras covering entrances and the server area — and confirm it's actually monitored, not just installed.

controls.requirement: Escort visitors and monitor visitor activity.

guidance.plain: Visitors — vendors, contractors, and guests — are escorted and their activity is watched while they're in areas with systems or CUI; they don't wander unaccompanied. Note: this control cannot be placed on a POA&M — it must be met at assessment, not deferred.
guidance.evidence: A visitor policy; a visitor sign-in log; evidence of the escort practice (visitor badges/passes, escort assignments).
guidance.quick_win: Start a visitor sign-in log and require staff to escort every visitor in sensitive areas — and because this can't go on a POA&M, close it now rather than deferring it.

controls.requirement: Maintain audit logs of physical access.

guidance.plain: You keep records of physical access to the areas housing systems and CUI — who entered and when. That can be badge-reader logs or a manual entry log for the server room. Note: this control cannot be placed on a POA&M — it must be met at assessment, not deferred.
guidance.evidence: Physical access logs (badge-system reports or a manual sign-in log for controlled areas), retained over time.
guidance.quick_win: Start keeping a physical access log for your server/network room today — a badge-reader report or a sign-in sheet — since this control can't be deferred on a POA&M.

controls.requirement: Control and manage physical access devices.

guidance.plain: Physical access devices — keys, badges, access cards, and keypad codes — are controlled and managed: you know who holds them, collect them back when someone leaves, and change codes or rekey locks when needed. Note: this control cannot be placed on a POA&M — it must be met at assessment, not deferred.
guidance.evidence: A key/badge issuance-and-return log; a current list of who holds keys, cards, and codes; evidence that codes are changed or keys collected when someone departs.
guidance.quick_win: Make a list of every key, badge, and door code and who holds each, and add a step to collect them back at offboarding — this one must be met, not deferred.

controls.short_title: Safeguard CUI at alternate work sites
controls.requirement: Enforce safeguarding measures for CUI at alternate work sites.

guidance.plain: When people handle CUI away from the office — home offices or other remote sites — the same kinds of safeguards apply: secured devices, locked storage, protected connections. Remote work doesn't loosen CUI protection.
guidance.evidence: A telework/remote-work policy covering CUI safeguards; evidence of enforced controls on remote devices (encryption, VPN, screen lock); remote-worker acknowledgments.
guidance.quick_win: Write a short alternate-work-site policy requiring the same safeguards — encrypted device, VPN, locked storage, screen lock — for anyone handling CUI off-site. Where that encryption is what's protecting the CUI, it must be FIPS-validated (3.13.11).

controls.requirement: Periodically assess the risk to organizational operations (including mission, functions, image, or reputation), organizational assets, and individuals, resulting from the operation of organizational systems and the associated processing, storage, or transmission of CUI.

guidance.plain: On a regular schedule you assess the risks to your operation from running systems that store, process, or transmit CUI — what could go wrong, how likely it is, and what the impact would be — and you write it down. 'We know our risks' in someone's head doesn't count; this is a documented, periodic assessment.
guidance.evidence: A dated risk assessment report covering threats to CUI systems, likelihood, and impact; a defined frequency (e.g., annual and on significant change); evidence it feeds decisions (ties to your POA&M/remediation).
guidance.quick_win: Complete a first written risk assessment from a simple template — list your CUI systems, the top threats to each, and their likelihood/impact — and set a recurring date to refresh it.

controls.requirement: Scan for vulnerabilities in organizational systems and applications periodically and when new vulnerabilities affecting those systems and applications are identified.

guidance.plain: Something regularly scans your systems for known security holes, and you look at the results. 'Our IT guy keeps an eye on things' is not a scan.
guidance.evidence: Recent vulnerability scan reports with dates; a note on scan frequency.
guidance.quick_win: Microsoft Defender's built-in vulnerability management or a free scanner tier gets you a first real report this week.

controls.requirement: Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments.

guidance.plain: You fix the security weaknesses you find, prioritized by the risk they pose — the serious, exploitable holes get remediated first, on a defined timeline, rather than everything sitting untouched. This closes the loop on your scanning (3.11.2) and risk assessment (3.11.1): findings actually get acted on.
guidance.evidence: Remediation records tied to scan/risk findings (what was fixed, when, and the risk-based priority); a remediation policy with timelines by severity; a POA&M or tracker showing open items and target dates.
guidance.quick_win: Take your latest vulnerability scan, rank the findings by risk, and set remediation deadlines by severity (e.g., critical within X days) — then track them to closure.

controls.requirement: Periodically assess the security controls in organizational systems to determine if the controls are effective in their application.

guidance.plain: On a set schedule you actually check whether your security controls are working — a self-assessment against the requirements to see if each one is effective in practice, not a one-time setup you never revisit. The goal is to find your own gaps before an assessor (or an attacker) does.
guidance.evidence: A dated self-assessment report scored against NIST SP 800-171; a defined assessment frequency (e.g., annually); notes on who performed it and how each area was checked (examine documents, interview staff, test the control).
guidance.quick_win: Run a full self-assessment across all 110 requirements now, date it, and put next year's assessment on the calendar — this tool's scoring gives you that starting baseline.

controls.requirement: Develop and implement plans of action designed to correct deficiencies and reduce or eliminate vulnerabilities in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: For every gap your assessment turns up, you write down how you'll fix it — the specific corrective action, who owns it, and a target date — and then you work the list until items close. That fix-it list is your Plan of Action & Milestones (POA&M), and 'implement' means you're genuinely reducing deficiencies, not just logging them.
guidance.evidence: A POA&M listing each open deficiency with a planned remediation action, a responsible owner, a target completion date, and current status; evidence that earlier items were actually closed out over time.
guidance.quick_win: Use this tool's POA&M tab to turn each open gap into a dated action item with an owner, then start closing the cheapest ones — note that a few specific controls can't be deferred to a POA&M and must be met outright (the tool flags them).

controls.requirement: Monitor security controls on an ongoing basis to ensure the continued effectiveness of the controls.

guidance.plain: Beyond the scheduled self-assessment, you keep an eye on your controls all year — confirming patches stay current, MFA stays enforced, logs get reviewed, and scans keep running — so you catch a control that quietly broke instead of discovering it at the next annual review. Continuous means a frequency high enough to actually support decisions, not just once a year.
guidance.evidence: A documented approach to ongoing monitoring (what you watch and how often) plus recurring proof it operates — vulnerability-scan schedules, dashboard/alert reviews, periodic log-review records, and configuration-drift checks.
guidance.quick_win: Pick a handful of high-value signals (patch compliance, MFA coverage, AV/EDR health) and set a recurring calendar check to review them — ongoing monitoring is a cadence you keep, not a product you buy.

controls.requirement: Develop, document, and periodically update system security plans that describe system boundaries, system environments of operation, how security requirements are implemented, and the relationships with or connections to other systems.

guidance.plain: The System Security Plan is the master document describing your environment and how each requirement is met. Under the DoD methodology, without an SSP there is no score at all — the assessment cannot be completed.
guidance.evidence: The SSP itself, with a revision date within the last year.
guidance.quick_win: Start from a free SSP template and fill in your real environment — an honest draft beats a blank page in every scenario.

controls.requirement: Monitor, control, and protect communications (i.e., information transmitted or received by organizational systems) at the external boundaries and key internal boundaries of organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You have a controlled, monitored edge to your network — a real managed firewall at the internet boundary (and at key internal boundaries, like around wherever CUI lives) that controls and inspects what crosses. Not just the ISP's box on default settings.
guidance.evidence: Firewall configuration/ruleset at the boundary; evidence of boundary monitoring/logging (firewall logs, IDS/IPS); a network diagram marking the external and key internal boundaries.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm a managed firewall sits at your internet edge with logging on, and identify the boundary around wherever CUI lives so you can protect it specifically.

controls.requirement: Employ architectural designs, software development techniques, and systems engineering principles that promote effective information security within organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Security is built into how your systems are designed and set up — you follow recognized secure-design and hardening principles (segmentation, least functionality, defense in depth) rather than bolting security on afterward. For a small shop this is largely documenting that you follow a known baseline/reference architecture.
guidance.evidence: A documented system/security architecture, or a reference to the secure baseline you follow (e.g., CIS Benchmarks, vendor security baselines); evidence that changes consider security impact.
guidance.quick_win: Write a short architecture note describing how your network is segmented and which hardening baseline you follow — that's the core artifact assessors look for here.

controls.requirement: Separate user functionality from system management functionality.

guidance.plain: The tools and interfaces used to administer systems are kept separate from the ones regular users use — admins manage systems through dedicated consoles and admin accounts, not from the same interface a standard user has.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that management interfaces are separate from user functionality (separate admin portals, a jump host, distinct admin accounts).
guidance.quick_win: Make sure system administration happens through dedicated admin consoles/accounts, not mixed into everyday user desktops.

controls.requirement: Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.

guidance.plain: Shared system resources (memory, temporary storage, scratch space) don't leak leftover data from one user or process to the next. For a small shop this is mostly a matter of running current, supported, patched operating systems, which clear resources between uses by default.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of supported/patched operating systems with default object-reuse protections in place; no shared scratch areas that expose residual data.
guidance.quick_win: Keep systems on current, supported, patched OS versions — object-reuse protection is built in; the real risk is unsupported or unpatched systems.

controls.requirement: Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks.

guidance.plain: Anything the public can reach — a web server or a public-facing service — sits on a separate network segment (a DMZ) walled off from your internal network, so a compromise of the public thing doesn't hand over the inside.
guidance.evidence: A network diagram showing public-facing components in a separate subnet/DMZ; firewall rules isolating that segment from the internal network.
guidance.quick_win: If you host anything publicly reachable, put it on a separate subnet/VLAN with firewall rules blocking it from initiating connections into your internal network. If you host nothing public, document that.

controls.short_title: Deny by default, allow by exception
controls.requirement: Deny network communications traffic by default and allow network communications traffic by exception (i.e., deny all, permit by exception).

guidance.plain: Your firewall blocks all traffic by default and only allows what you've explicitly permitted — both inbound and, importantly, outbound. The opposite (allow-all, block-a-few) is the common weak default this control exists to fix.
guidance.evidence: A firewall ruleset ending in an explicit default-deny; a documented list of explicitly-permitted flows; evidence that outbound traffic is also restricted, not just inbound.
guidance.quick_win: Add an explicit default-deny at the end of your firewall rules and review outbound rules — most shops allow all outbound, which this control expects you to tighten.

controls.requirement: Prevent remote devices from simultaneously establishing non-remote connections with organizational systems and communicating via some other connection to resources in external networks (i.e., split tunneling).

guidance.plain: When a device is connected to your network remotely (over VPN), it can't simultaneously bridge to the open internet in a way that routes around your protections — the VPN forces that device's traffic through your controls instead of out a second, uncontrolled path.
guidance.evidence: VPN configuration enforcing full-tunnel, or otherwise preventing split tunneling, for remote devices.
guidance.quick_win: Set your VPN to full-tunnel (or explicitly disable split tunneling) so remote sessions can't bridge your network to the open internet.

controls.requirement: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure of CUI during transmission unless otherwise protected by alternative physical safeguards.

guidance.plain: CUI moving over networks is encrypted — TLS for web and email paths, VPN for site-to-site. If a file with CUI travels in the clear, this fails.
guidance.evidence: Configuration screenshots showing TLS enforcement / VPN settings for paths that carry CUI.
guidance.quick_win: Force TLS on your email connectors and require the VPN for any remote path that touches CUI.

controls.requirement: Terminate network connections associated with communications sessions at the end of the sessions or after a defined period of inactivity.

guidance.plain: Network sessions get torn down when they end or after a period of inactivity — connections don't linger open indefinitely (VPN idle timeout, server and firewall session timeouts).
guidance.evidence: Configuration showing network/session idle timeouts (VPN, servers, firewall session tables).
guidance.quick_win: Set idle timeouts on your VPN and key network services so idle connections drop automatically.

controls.requirement: Establish and manage cryptographic keys for cryptography employed in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: The keys and certificates behind your encryption are managed properly — generated, stored, rotated, and retired securely — not left in default states or shared insecurely.
guidance.evidence: A key/certificate management process, or evidence of managed keys (a certificate inventory with expiry dates, protected key storage, a rotation practice).
guidance.quick_win: Inventory your certificates and encryption keys with their expiry dates, and confirm private keys aren't sitting in shared or unprotected locations.

controls.requirement: Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.

guidance.plain: Where encryption protects CUI, the cryptographic module must be FIPS-validated — using a strong algorithm isn't enough; the module needs a CMVP certificate. Partial credit: encryption in use but not FIPS-validated loses 3 points instead of 5.
guidance.evidence: List of encryption in use with CMVP certificate numbers (e.g., BitLocker in FIPS mode).
guidance.quick_win: Inventory where CUI is encrypted today, then check each product's FIPS validation status on the NIST CMVP database.

controls.requirement: Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and provide indication of devices in use to users present at the device.

guidance.plain: Cameras, microphones, and conferencing devices can't be turned on remotely without the people in the room knowing — there's a clear indication (a light or on-screen prompt) when they're active. This prevents covert activation.
guidance.evidence: Configuration/policy preventing remote activation of cameras/mics; evidence of in-use indicators; conferencing application settings.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm your collaboration apps show a clear in-use indicator and can't silently enable cameras/mics; set policies accordingly, or physically disable/cover devices where not needed.

controls.requirement: Control and monitor the use of mobile code.

guidance.plain: "Mobile code" — things like Office macros, JavaScript, ActiveX, and Java applets that run automatically — is controlled: you decide what's allowed to run and block risky types, especially from untrusted sources.
guidance.evidence: Policy and technical controls for mobile code (macro-blocking policies, browser/endpoint settings restricting active content).
guidance.quick_win: Block Office macros that come from the internet (a standard Microsoft 365 / Group Policy setting) — that's the highest-value mobile-code control for most shops.

controls.requirement: Control and monitor the use of Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technologies.

guidance.plain: If you use internet voice/phone systems (VoIP, or Teams/Zoom calling), their use is controlled and monitored — configured securely, not left wide open. If you use no VoIP at all, record that in your SSP.
guidance.evidence: VoIP configuration/policy showing secured, monitored use (segmentation, authentication), or SSP documentation that no VoIP is used.
guidance.quick_win: If you use VoIP, confirm it's on a controlled/segmented setup with authentication; if you don't, write a one-line SSP note that VoIP isn't used.

controls.requirement: Protect the authenticity of communications sessions.

guidance.plain: Communications sessions are protected so they can't be hijacked or tampered with mid-stream — which in practice comes from using authenticated, encrypted protocols (TLS, IPsec) rather than unauthenticated ones.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that sessions use authenticated/encrypted protocols (TLS everywhere, IPsec for site-to-site); configuration disabling weak or unauthenticated protocols.
guidance.quick_win: Enforce TLS on internal and external web/services and retire legacy unauthenticated protocols — that's what protects session authenticity in practice.

controls.requirement: Protect the confidentiality of CUI at rest.

guidance.plain: CUI sitting in storage — on drives, servers, databases, and backups — is protected so someone who gets the disk or file can't read the CUI. Encryption at rest is the usual mechanism (offline or physically-secured storage is an accepted alternative where encryption isn't feasible). When you use encryption to protect CUI, it must be FIPS-validated — that requirement comes from 3.13.11.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of encryption at rest wherever CUI is stored (BitLocker/volume encryption, database or file encryption), including backups; for the encryption used to protect CUI, the FIPS 140 CMVP validation certificate (certificate number) for the module. Where encryption isn't used, evidence of the alternative protection (e.g., secured offline storage).
guidance.quick_win: Turn on and enforce BitLocker/volume encryption everywhere CUI is stored, including backups, and record the module's CMVP certificate number so you can show the encryption is FIPS-validated (3.13.11).

controls.requirement: Identify, report, and correct system flaws in a timely manner.

guidance.plain: Security patches get applied on a schedule with deadlines — not 'when we get around to it.' You can show when a given patch landed.
guidance.evidence: Patch compliance report from your update tool; written patching cadence with deadlines.
guidance.quick_win: Set automatic updates with a defined maximum deferral and export your first compliance report.

controls.requirement: Provide protection from malicious code at designated locations within organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Anti-malware runs on every machine, it's centrally visible, and nobody can quietly turn it off.
guidance.evidence: Coverage report from your AV/EDR console showing all endpoints protected and current.
guidance.quick_win: Pull the coverage report and chase the stragglers — the gap machines are the whole risk.

controls.requirement: Monitor system security alerts and advisories and take action in response.

guidance.plain: You stay plugged into security alerts and advisories — from CISA, your software and hardware vendors, and your security tools — and when one is relevant you act on it (patch, mitigate, or record why it doesn't apply to you). Receiving the email isn't the control; doing something about it is.
guidance.evidence: Subscriptions to alert sources (CISA/US-CERT alerts, vendor security bulletins, your Defender/EDR advisory feed); a record showing a specific advisory was reviewed and what action followed.
guidance.quick_win: Subscribe to CISA alerts and your key vendors' security bulletins, assign one person to triage them, and log the action taken on the next advisory that affects you.

controls.requirement: Update malicious code protection mechanisms when new releases are available.

guidance.plain: Your anti-malware keeps itself current — engine and definition/signature updates roll out automatically and promptly as the vendor releases them — so protection isn't running on last month's threat intelligence.
guidance.evidence: AV/EDR configuration showing automatic engine and signature updates enabled; a console report showing definition versions are current across all endpoints.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm your AV/EDR policy is set to auto-update engine and signatures, then pull a coverage report to catch any endpoint stuck on stale definitions.

controls.requirement: Perform periodic scans of organizational systems and real-time scans of files from external sources as files are downloaded, opened, or executed.

guidance.plain: Two kinds of scanning are running: scheduled full scans of your systems on a regular cadence, and real-time (on-access) scanning that checks files from outside as they're downloaded, opened, or executed — so a threat is caught the moment it arrives, not just at the next weekly scan.
guidance.evidence: AV/EDR settings showing real-time / on-access protection enabled and a periodic scheduled scan configured; scan history or reports confirming both actually run.
guidance.quick_win: Verify real-time protection is on across every endpoint and add a scheduled periodic scan if one isn't set — both are usually a single toggle in your AV/EDR policy.

controls.requirement: Monitor organizational systems, including inbound and outbound communications traffic, to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks.

guidance.plain: You watch network traffic — both what comes in and what goes out — for signs of an attack, rather than relying only on antivirus sitting on the endpoints. Outbound matters as much as inbound: malware calling home or CUI leaving the network is often the first real signal. In practice this means a firewall or security tool with intrusion detection/prevention, EDR with network detection, or a monitored log/SIEM feed that raises alerts a person actually reviews.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of traffic monitoring covering both inbound and outbound flows — IDS/IPS enabled on the firewall, EDR network detection, or a SIEM/log pipeline with detection rules; sample alerts plus a record that someone reviews them and responds.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on the intrusion-detection/IPS and outbound-threat features your firewall or EDR likely already includes, route their alerts to a monitored inbox, and make sure someone reviews and acts on them — unreviewed alerts don't satisfy this control.

controls.requirement: Identify unauthorized use of organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You can spot when your systems are being used by someone or something that shouldn't be — a sign-in from an unexpected place, an account doing things it never normally does, activity at odd hours. This builds on your logging: you define what authorized use looks like and watch for what falls outside it.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that you monitor for unauthorized use — identity risk / unusual-sign-in alerts (e.g., Entra ID), EDR or log-based alerts on anomalous activity, a defined notion of authorized use, and who reviews the exceptions.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on your identity provider's risky-sign-in and unusual-activity alerts (e.g., Entra ID) and decide who reviews them — that's the fastest way to actually detect unauthorized use.
